Name: International Mathematical Olympiad (IMO)
Eligibility: National team selection required; pre-university students
Description: Annual international mathematics competition for pre-university students, held since 1959; national teams compete in two exam papers.
Cost: Free (national selection process varies by country)
Notes: Team selection is handled by each country's national olympiad committee, not centrally; host country/dates change yearly.

Name: International Physics Olympiad (IPhO)
Eligibility: National team selection required; pre-university students
Description: Annual international physics competition for secondary school students, held since 1967.
Cost: Free (national selection process varies by country)
Notes: Selection handled nationally; host country/dates change yearly.

Name: International Chemistry Olympiad (IChO)
Organizer: International Chemistry Olympiad International Information Centre
Eligibility: National team selection required; secondary school students
Description: Annual international chemistry competition held since 1968, combining theoretical and experimental exams.
Cost: Free (national selection process varies by country)
Notes: Selection handled nationally; host country/dates change yearly.

Name: International Olympiad in Informatics (IOI)
Eligibility: National team selection required; pre-university students
Description: Annual international programming/algorithms competition for secondary school students, held since 1989.
Cost: Free (national selection process varies by country)
Notes: Selection handled nationally; host country/dates change yearly.

Name: International Biology Olympiad (IBO)
Eligibility: National team selection required; pre-university students under 20
Description: Annual international biology competition for secondary school students, held since 1990.
Cost: Free (national selection process varies by country)
Notes: Selection handled nationally; host country/dates change yearly.

Name: Regeneron International Science and Engineering Fair (ISEF)
Eligibility: Must first win at a Society-affiliated local/regional/state/national science fair
Description: World's largest international pre-college science competition; finalists qualify through affiliated fairs in 60+ countries, regions, and territories.
Cost: Free to compete (affiliate fair/travel costs vary)
Notes: Entry is via affiliated local fairs, not direct application; affiliate fair deadlines vary by region.

Name: John Locke Institute Essay Competition (Global Essay Prize)
Eligibility: Open globally; no age restriction stated
Description: Annual essay competition across philosophy, politics, economics, history, psychology, theology, and law; participants from 100+ countries.
Notes: Deadline changes annually; check official site for current cycle.

Name: World Scholar's Cup
Eligibility: Teams of three students; three age divisions
Description: Global academic competition combining a quiz bowl, collaborative writing, team debate, and a Tournament of Champions at Yale University; regional rounds in 70+ countries.
Notes: Regional round dates/fees vary by location; check local round listing.

Name: Research Science Institute (RSI)
Organizer: Center for Excellence in Education (CEE), hosted at MIT
Country_Region: United States (international students apply via home-country selection)
Eligibility: US students with one year left before high school graduation (seniors not eligible); international applicants apply through their own country's selection process
Description: Free 6-week on-campus summer research program combining STEM coursework with mentored research; approximately 100 students selected annually.
Notes: International admission runs through separate national selection procedures with their own timelines.

Name: PRIMES (Program for Research in Mathematics, Engineering and Science for High School Students)
Eligibility: High school sophomores/juniors; specific grades vary by track (MIT PRIMES, PRIMES-USA, PRIMES Circle, Yulia's Dream)
Description: Year-long mentored research program in mathematics, computer science, and computational biology for high school students.
Notes: Multiple tracks with different eligibility/geography; see official site for the specific track.

Name: Simons Summer Research Program
Eligibility: US citizens/permanent residents; high school juniors, at least 16 by program start, nominated by their school
Description: 7-week mentored research program pairing approximately 40 selected high school students with Stony Brook University faculty.
Notes: Requires school nomination (max 2 per school); highly selective (~5% acceptance rate).

Name: Summer Science Program (SSP)
Eligibility: High school juniors
Description: 5-week residential summer research program in astrophysics, biochemistry, bacterial genomics, or synthetic chemistry, held on partner college campuses.
Notes: Cost varies by track/campus; need-based financial aid available.

Name: Yale Young Global Scholars (YYGS)
Eligibility: High school students from around the world (150+ countries)
Description: Two-week residential summer sessions at Yale across three tracks: Innovations in Science & Technology, Politics of Law & Economics, and Solving Global Challenges.
Cost: Paid (need-based financial aid and scholarships available)
Notes: Tuition varies by session; see site for current pricing and aid options.

Name: MITES (MIT Introduction to Technology, Engineering, and Science)
Country_Region: United States (MITES Saturdays limited to Boston/Cambridge/Lawrence, MA)
Eligibility: US citizens/permanent residents, 11th grade, for MITES Summer/Semester; MITES Saturdays limited to Boston-area public school students grades 7-10
Description: Free residential (Summer) or remote (Semester) STEM enrichment program at MIT for underrepresented high school students.
Notes: Multiple program tracks (Summer/Semester/Saturdays) with different eligibility and formats.

Name: Davis United World College Scholars Program
Organizer: Administered by Middlebury College (founded by Shelby Davis and Phil Geier)
Country_Region: International (for UWC graduates attending US universities)
Eligibility: Graduates of the 18 United World College (UWC) high schools
Description: Need-based financial aid for UWC graduates pursuing higher education at 100+ partner colleges/universities in the United States.
Notes: Only open to UWC graduates, not the general public.

Name: QuestBridge National College Match
Eligibility: High-achieving high school seniors from low-income backgrounds
Description: Matches selected students with full four-year scholarships at QuestBridge partner colleges.
Notes: Applicants rank partner colleges; matches are binding at some partners, similar to Early Decision.

Name: Center for Talented Youth (CTY)
Eligibility: Grades 2-12; qualification via standardized test scores (e.g., 95th+ percentile for Advanced CTY Level)
Description: Academic enrichment courses (in-person and online) for advanced learners, spanning STEM and humanities subjects.
Cost: Paid, varies by course (e.g., ~$7,801 for a residential course); need-based financial aid available; $15 application fee
Notes: Tuition varies significantly by course/length; see course catalog for exact pricing.

Name: Stanford Online High School
Eligibility: Academically advanced students, grades 7-12
Description: Accredited, fully online private high school with live seminar-style classes; awards approximately $2.5M annually in financial aid.
Notes: Full diploma-granting school, not a single course; admissions is selective.

Name: Conrad Challenge
Eligibility: Ages 13-18, teams of 2-5 students
Description: Multi-stage innovation/entrepreneurship competition where student teams design solutions to real-world problems, culminating in an in-person Innovation Summit.
Cost: Free to start (Activation/Lean Canvas stages); $499/team for Innovation Stage; $499/person for in-person Summit; financial aid available
Notes: Costs apply only at later stages; discounts available for Title I schools.

Name: Technovation Girls
Eligibility: Girls/underrepresented genders in tech, team-based
Description: Global technology entrepreneurship program where student teams build mobile/AI solutions to community problems.
Notes: Program registration is managed via technovationchallenge.org; confirm current eligibility there.

Name: The Concord Review
Eligibility: Secondary school students (history research papers)
Description: Quarterly journal publishing academic history research papers written by secondary school students; also operates the National Writing Board assessment service.
Notes: Typical submission is a long-form (~5,000+ word) history research paper.

Name: Journal of Student Research (High School Edition)
Eligibility: First author must be a high school student (grades 9-12) with a listed teacher/advisor
Description: Peer-reviewed, open-access multidisciplinary journal publishing high school, undergraduate, and graduate student research (AP Research, IB, honors research, etc.).
Cost: Paid Article Processing Charge: $50 at submission + $299 after acceptance
Notes: Double-blind peer review; APC charged in two phases.

Name: Harvard Model United Nations (HMUN)
Eligibility: High school students; delegations from 60+ countries
Description: Large in-person Model UN conference held in Boston, run by Harvard undergraduates, simulating international diplomacy committees.
Notes: Held annually in late January; check official site for current session dates/fees.
